## IMPROVED DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We explore denoising diffusion probabilistic models, a class of generative models which have recently been shown to produce excellent samples in the image and audio domains. While these models produce excellent samples, it has yet to be shown that they can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. We show that, with several small modifications, diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods in the image domain while maintaining high sample quality. Additionally, our models allow for sampling with an order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps with only a modest difference in sample quality. Finally, we explore how sample quality and log-likelihood scale with the number of diffusion steps and the amount of model capacity. We conclude that denoising diffusion probabilistic models are a promising class of generative models with excellent scaling properties and sample quality.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) introduced diffusion probabilistic models ("diffusion models" for brevity), a class of generative models which match a data distribution by learning to reverse a gradual, multi-step noising process. More recently, Ho et al. (2020) showed an equivalence between these models and score based generative models (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), which learn a gradient of the log-density of the data distribution using denoising score matching (Hyvärinen, 2005). It has recently been shown that this class of models can produce high-quality images (Ho et al., 2020; Song & Ermon, 2020; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2020) and audio (Chen et al., 2020b; Kong et al., 2020), but it has yet to be shown that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. Furthermore, while Ho et al. (2020) showed extremely good results on the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) and LSUN (Yu et al., 2015) datasets, it is unclear how well diffusion models scale to datasets with higher diversity such as ImageNet. Finally, while Chen et al. (2020b) found that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio using a small number of sampling steps, it has yet to be shown that the same is true for images.

In this paper, we show that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods while maintaining good sample quality, even on high-diversity datasets like ImageNet. Additionally, we show that our improved models can produce competitive samples an order of magnitude faster than those from Ho et al. (2020). We achieve these results by combining a simple reparameterization of the reverse process variance, a hybrid learning objective that combines the variational lower-bound with the simplified objective from Ho et al. (2020), and a novel noise schedule which allows the model to better leverage the entire diffusion process.

We find surprisingly that, with our hybrid objective, our models obtain better log-likelihoods than those obtained by optimizing the log-likelihood directly, and discover that the latter objective has much more gradient noise during training. We show that a simple importance sampling technique reduces this noise and allows us to achieve better log-likelihoods than with the hybrid objective. Using our trained models, we study how sample quality and log-likelihood change as we adjust the number of diffusion steps used at sampling time. We demonstrate that our improved models allow us to use an order of magnitude fewer steps at test time with only a modest change in sample quality and log-likelihood, thus speeding up sampling for use in practical applications.
Finally, we evaluate the performance of these models as we increase model size, and observe trends that suggest predictable improvements in performance as we increase training compute.

## 2 DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

We briefly review the formulation of diffusion models from Ho et al. (2020). This formulation makes various simplifying assumptions, such as a fixed noising process _q_ which adds diagonal Gaussian noise at each timestep. For a more general derivation, see Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015).

## 3 IMPROVING THE LOG-LIKELIHOOD

While Ho et al. (2020) found that diffusion models can generate high-fidelity samples according to FID (Heusel et al., 2017) and Inception Score (Salimans et al., 2016), they were unable to achieve competitive log-likelihoods with these models. Log-likelihood is a widely used metric in generative modeling, and it is generally believed that optimizing log-likelihood forces generative models to capture all of the modes of the data distribution (Razavi et al., 2019). Additionally, recent work (Henighan et al., 2020) has shown that small improvements in log-likelihood can have a dramatic impact on sample quality and learnt feature representations. Thus, it is important to explore why diffusion models seem to perform poorly on this metric, since this may suggest a fundamental shortcoming such as bad mode coverage. This section explores several modifications to the algorithm described in Section 2 that, when combined, allow diffusion models to achieve much better log-likelihoods on image datasets, suggesting that these models enjoy the same benefits as other likelihood-based generative models.

To study the effects of different modifications, we train fixed model architectures with fixed hyperparameters (Appendix A) on the ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 (van den Oord et al., 2016a) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. While CIFAR-10 has seen more usage for this class of models, we chose to study ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 as well because it provides a good trade-off between diversity and resolution, allowing us to train models quickly without worrying about overfitting. Additionally, ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 has been studied extensively in the context of generative modeling (van den Oord et al., 2016b; Menick & Kalchbrenner, 2018; Child et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2020), allowing us to compare diffusion models directly to many other generative models.

The setup from Ho et al. (2020) (optimizing _L_ simple while setting _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[and] _[ T]_[=][1000][) achieves] a log-likelihood of **3.99 bits/dim** on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 after 200K training iterations. We found in early experiments that we could get a boost in log-likelihood by increasing _T_ from 1000 to 4000; with this change, the log-likelihood improves to **3.77 bits/dim** . For the remainder of this section, we use _T_ = 4000, but we explore this choice in Section 4.

This suggests that, in the limit of infinite diffusion steps, the choice of _σt_ might not matter at all for sample quality. In other words, as we add more diffusion steps, the model mean _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) determines the distribution much more than Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

While the above argument suggests that fixing _σt_ is a reasonable choice for the sake of sample quality, it says nothing about log-likelihood. In fact, Figure 1b shows that the first few steps of the diffusion process contribute the most to the variational lower bound. Thus, it seems likely that we could improve log-likelihood by using a better choice of Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ). To achieve this, we must learn Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) without the instabilities encountered by Ho et al. (2020).

Since Figure 1a shows that the reasonable range for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) is very small, it would be hard for a neural network to predict Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly, even in the log domain, as observed by Ho et al. (2020). Instead, we found it better to parameterize the variance as an interpolation between _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ in the log domain. In particular, our model outputs a vector _v_ containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows:

We did not apply any constraints on _v_ , theoretically allowing the model to predict variances outside of the interpolated range. However, we did not observe the network doing this in practice, suggesting that the bounds for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) are indeed expressive enough.

Since _L_ simple doesn’t depend on Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ), we define a new hybrid objective:

For our experiments, we set _λ_ = 0 _._ 001 to prevent _L_ vlb from overwhelming _L_ simple. Along this same line of reasoning, we also apply a stop-gradient to the _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) output for the _L_ vlb term. This way, _L_ vlb can guide Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) while _L_ simple is still the main source of influence over _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

In particular, we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5. We chose to use _cos_[2] in particular because it is a common mathematical function with the shape we were looking for. This choice was arbitrary, and we expect that many other functions with similar shapes would work as well.

## 3.3 REDUCING GRADIENT NOISE

We expected to achieve the best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb directly, rather than by optimizing _L_ hybrid. However, we were surprised to find that _L_ vlb was actually quite difficult to optimize in practice, at least on the diverse ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 dataset. Figure 4a shows the learning curves for both _L_ vlb and _L_ hybrid. Both curves are noisy, but the hybrid objective clearly achieves better log-likelihoods on the training set given the same amount of training time.

We hypothesized that the gradient of _L_ vlb was much noisier than that of _L_ hybrid. We confirmed this by evaluating the gradient noise scales (McCandlish et al., 2018) for models trained with both objectives, as shown in Figure 4b. Thus, we sought out a way to reduce the variance of _L_ vlb in order to optimize directly for log-likelihood.

Noting that different terms of _L_ vlb have greatly different magnitudes (Figure 1b), we hypothesized that sampling _t_ uniformly causes unnecessary noise in the _L_ vlb objective. To address this, we employ importance sampling:

Since _E_ [ _L_[2] _t_[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a][history] of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample _t_ uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every _t ∈_ [0 _, T −_ 1]. With this importance sampled objective, we are able to achieve our best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb.[1] This can be seen in Figure 4a as the " _Lvlb_ (resampled)" curve. The figure also shows that the importance sampled objective is considerably less noisy than the original, uniformly sampled objective.

## 3.4 RESULTS AND ABLATIONS

In this section, we ablate the changes we have made to achieve better log-likelihoods. Table 1 summarizes the results of our ablations on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64, and Table 2 shows them for CIFAR10. We also trained our best ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 models for 1.5M iterations, and report these results as well. Based on the results, we recommend always using the cosine schedule, and the _L_ hybrid objective in most cases. If one is only optimizing for likelihood and not sample quality, the importance sampled _L_ vlb is the best objective to use.

## 4 IMPROVING SAMPLING SPEED

All of our models were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, and thus producing a single sample takes several minutes on a modern GPU. In this section, we explore how performance scales if we reduce the steps used during sampling, and find that our pre-trained models can produce high-quality samples with many fewer diffusion steps than they were trained with without any fine-tuning. Reducing the steps in this way makes it possible to sample from our models in a number of seconds rather than minutes, and greatly improves the practical applicability of image diffusion models.

For a model trained with _T_ diffusion steps, we would typically sample using the same set of _t_ values (1 _,_ 2 _, ..., T_ ) as used during training. However, it is also possible to sample using an arbitrary set of _t_ values. We define a sequence _S_ of _t_ values to use for sampling, such as a strided schedule like ¯ _S_ schedule= (1 _,_ 3 _α_ ¯ _,S_ 5 _t, ..., T_ , which can be used to obtain corresponding sampling variances _−_ 1). Given the training noise schedule _αt_ , we can obtain the sampling noise

We can compute _p_ ( _xSt−_ 1 _|xSt_ ) as _N_ ( _µθ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) _,_ Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ )). Note that Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) is parameterized as a range between _βSt_ and _β_[˜] _St_ so it will automatically be rescaled for the shorter diffusion process.

To evaluate sample quality for reduced numbers of sampling steps, we use a stride _K_ over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from _T_ to _T/K_ . In Figures 5a and 5c, we evaluate FIDs for an _L_ hybrid model and an _L_ simple model that were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, using 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, and 4000 sampling steps. We do this for multiple checkpoints throughout training. We find that the _L_ simple model suffers much more in sample quality when using a reduced number of sampling steps, whereas our _L_ hybrid model maintains sample quality. Furthermore, we find that using more sampling steps becomes increasingly beneficial throughout training. However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs for our fully trained models.

In initial experiments, we found that although constant striding did not significantly affect FID, it drastically reduced log-likelihood. To address this, we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule. In Figures 5b and 5d we present log-likelihoods with this modified strided schedule.

To measure how performance scales with compute, we train four different models on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 with the _L_ hybrid objective described in Section 3.1. To change model capacity, we apply a depth multiplier across all layers, such that the first layer has either 64, 96, 128, or 192 channels. Note that our previous experiments used 128 channels in the first layer. Since the depth of each layer affects the scale of the initial weights, we scale the Adam learning rate for each model by 1 _/[√]_ channel multiplier, such that the 128 channel model has a learning rate of 0.0001 (as in our other experiments).

Figure 6a and 6b show how FID and NLL improve relative to compute. These plots reveal that, to achieve optimal performance for a given amount of compute, it often makes sense to train a larger model for fewer iterations, rather than training a smaller model to convergence. We note that these models do not achieve optimal log-likelihoods because they were trained with our _L_ hybrid objective and not directly with _L_ vlb to keep both good log-likelihoods and sample quality. The x-axis in both figures is the theoretical amount of training compute, assuming full hardware utilization.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Chen et al. (2020b) and Kong et al. (2020) are two recent works that use diffusion models to produce high fidelity audio conditioned on mel-spectrograms. Concurrent to our work, Chen et al. (2020b) use a combination of improved schedule and _L_ 1 loss to allow sampling with fewer steps with very little reduction in sample quality. However, compared to our unconditional image generation task, their generative task has a strong input conditioning signal provided by the mel-spectrograms, and we hypothesize that this makes it easier to sample with fewer diffusion steps.

Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2020) explored score matching in the image domain, and constructed an adversarial training objective to produce better _x_ 0 predictions. However, they found that choosing a better network architecture removed the need for this adversarial objective, suggesting that the adversarial objective is not necessary for powerful generative modeling.

## 7 CONCLUSION

We have shown that, with a few modifications, diffusion models can sample much faster and achieve better log-likelihoods with little impact on sample quality. Here we summarize our main findings:
- Our cosine noise schedule improves NLL (and sometimes FID) compared to the linear schedule from Ho et al. (2020).

- Learning Σ _θ_ using our parameterization and _L_ hybrid objective provides a good trade-off between NLL and FID. More importantly, it allows sampling with many fewer steps without decreased sample quality.
- One can optimize _L_ vlb directly using our importance sampling technique to achieve the best possible NLL at the expense of sample quality.

The combination of these results makes diffusion models an attractive choice for generative modeling, since they combine good log-likelihoods, high-quality samples, and fast sampling with a wellgrounded, stationary training objective. Furthermore, we have investigated how diffusion models scale with the amount of available training compute, and found that more training compute trivially leads to better sample quality and log-likelihood. These results indicate that diffusion models are a promising direction for future research, especially as the affordability of compute increases over time.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes several enhancements to Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) to improve their log-likelihood and sampling efficiency. The authors introduce a learned variance schedule using a hybrid loss function, a cosine-based noise schedule, and an importance sampling strategy to reduce gradient noise during maximum likelihood training.

The paper makes a significant contribution by bridging the gap between the high perceptual quality of diffusion models and the competitive log-likelihoods typically associated with autoregressive models. The introduction of learned variances is a particularly strong technical contribution that enables faster sampling without the quality degradation seen in previous fixed-variance implementations.

Overview issues:

- Trade-off between Log-Likelihood and Perceptual Quality: The results in Tables 1 and 2 reveal a persistent tension between optimizing for NLL and FID. While the importance-sampled L_vlb objective achieves the best log-likelihoods (3.53 on ImageNet 64x64), it significantly degrades the FID score (38.3 compared to 18.3 for the hybrid objective). The paper notes this discrepancy but does not provide a deep analysis of why maximum likelihood training leads to poorer visual samples in this framework. This suggests that the model might be allocating excessive capacity to imperceptible high-frequency noise to satisfy the NLL objective. A more detailed examination of the sample characteristics under L_vlb versus L_hybrid would clarify if this is a fundamental limitation of the likelihood objective for diffusion models.

- Sensitivity of the Hybrid Loss Weighting: In Section 3.1, the authors introduce a hybrid objective (Equation 17) with a fixed hyperparameter lambda set to 0.001. The paper claims this prevents L_vlb from overwhelming L_simple, but there is no ablation study or sensitivity analysis regarding this value across different datasets or resolutions. Given that the magnitudes of these loss terms change significantly during training (as shown in Figure 1b), a static weighting may not be optimal. The authors should provide evidence that this specific lambda generalizes or discuss how it should be scaled when applying the method to new domains or higher-resolution images.

- Lack of Comparison with Deterministic Sampling Baselines: Section 4 discusses improving sampling speed by striding the diffusion process, but it largely ignores the Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) framework, which is the standard baseline for accelerated sampling. While the authors show that learning the variance allows for fewer steps with 'modest' quality loss, they do not provide a head-to-head FID comparison against DDIM's deterministic sampling at the same step counts (e.g., 50 or 100 steps). Without this comparison, it is difficult to determine if the improvement in sampling speed stems from the learned variance parameterization or if it is simply a property of the strided schedule.

- Gradient Noise Analysis Limited to ImageNet 64x64: The importance sampling technique introduced in Section 3.3 is justified by the gradient noise scale analysis in Figure 4b. However, this analysis is only presented for ImageNet 64x64. Since the paper claims these modifications improve diffusion models generally, it is important to know if the gradient noise bottleneck is as severe on less diverse datasets like CIFAR-10 or higher-resolution datasets. If the noise scale varies significantly with data complexity, the necessity of the complex importance sampling scheme (Equation 19) might be overstated for simpler tasks.

- Ambiguity in the Parameterization of v: Equation 16 defines the learned variance as an interpolation between beta and beta-tilde using a model output vector v. The text states that the model outputs one component per dimension, but it is unclear if this refers to the spatial dimensions of the image or the latent feature dimensions of the U-Net. If v is spatially varying, it could introduce artifacts or overfit to local noise patterns. If it is a global scalar or per-channel vector, the capacity of the variance model is quite different. Clarifying the exact dimensionality of v and its architectural implementation in the U-Net is necessary for reproducibility.

- Missing verification of the cosine schedule on high-resolution data: The paper argues that the linear noise schedule is sub-optimal because it destroys information too quickly, particularly at higher resolutions where the end of the process is 'pure noise.' While this is demonstrated for ImageNet 64x64, the paper lacks a worked example or results for higher resolutions (e.g., 128x128 or 256x256) where this 'information destruction' problem should theoretically be even more pronounced. To show the result has bite, the authors should provide at least one set of NLL/FID metrics on ImageNet 128x128. This would confirm that the cosine schedule's benefit scales as resolution increases, rather than being an artifact of low-resolution binning.

- Absence of a worked example for the importance sampling history initialization: Section 3.3 introduces a dynamic importance sampling scheme that requires maintaining a history of the previous 10 values for each of the 4000 loss terms. The paper does not discuss the computational or memory overhead of this bookkeeping, nor how sensitive the final NLL is to the 'warm-up' period where $t$ is sampled uniformly. A small table showing the NLL achieved with different history lengths (e.g., 5, 10, 50) or different initialization strategies would provide the necessary implementation guidance. Without this, it is unclear if the 10-sample heuristic is a robust default or a tuned parameter.

- Underdeveloped implications of the stop-gradient in the hybrid objective: The authors apply a stop-gradient to the mean $\mu_\theta$ when calculating the $L_{vlb}$ term of the hybrid objective to ensure $L_{simple}$ remains the primary driver of the mean. This is a significant architectural choice that separates the learning of the first and second moments, yet the paper never evaluates a case where the gradient is allowed to flow through both. A comparison showing the training stability or NLL/FID results without this stop-gradient is needed to justify the claim that $L_{vlb}$ 'overwhelms' the mean. This would clarify whether the hybrid objective's success is due to the loss formulation itself or this specific gradient-routing trick.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Add a direct FID comparison between the proposed fast sampling (with learned sigmas) and the DDIM deterministic sampling baseline at 50 and 100 steps.

- Include a sensitivity analysis or ablation for the hybrid loss weight (lambda) to justify the choice of 0.001.

- Provide a qualitative or spectral analysis of samples generated by the L_vlb objective to explain why FID degrades while NLL improves.

- Clarify the spatial and channel dimensionality of the learned variance output v in Section 3.1.

comments.title: Mathematical error in x_0 reconstruction formula
comments.quote: x_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}}\left(x_{t}-\frac{\beta_{t}}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t}}}\epsilon\right)
comments.explanation: Equation 14 contains a derivation error for the reconstruction of x_0 from the noise. Given the forward process definition x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon, solving for x_0 should yield x_0 = (1 / sqrt(alpha_bar_t)) * (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon). Your version incorrectly uses sqrt(alpha_t) in the denominator and introduces an extra beta_t factor in the numerator. This formula would fail to recover the original image even with a perfect noise prediction. Please correct this to maintain consistency with the forward process defined in Equation 12.

comments.title: Contradiction between NLL optimization and sample quality claims
comments.quote: We show that, with several small modifications, diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods in the image domain while maintaining high sample quality.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that the proposed modifications maintain 'high sample quality' while achieving competitive log-likelihoods. However, the results in Table 1 and Table 2 show a significant trade-off: when optimizing for the best log-likelihood (3.53 on ImageNet 64x64 using L_vlb), the FID score degrades from 18.3 to 38.3. This doubling of the FID score contradicts the claim that high sample quality is maintained in the maximum likelihood regime. The text should clarify that maintaining quality is specific to the hybrid objective, which does not reach the most competitive log-likelihoods reported.

comments.explanation: The paper defines alpha_bar_t via a cosine function that reaches exactly 0 at t=T. However, it also defines beta_t = 1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_{t-1}. If beta_t is clipped to 0.999, then alpha_bar_t cannot reach 0, as alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar_{t-1}(1 - beta_t). If S_t is the t-th element of a sequence and we are sampling backwards, then S_t is the current timestep and S_{t-1} is the next (smaller) timestep. If S_t > S_{t-1}, then alpha_bar_{S_t} < alpha_bar_{S_{t-1}}, and the formula for beta_{S_t} works. However, the text defines S = (1, 3, 5, ...), where indices increase. This makes the sampling transition p(x_{S_{t-1}}|x_{S_t}) move from a larger index to a smaller one, which is the inverse of the training notation where beta_t defines the step from t-1 to t. comments.explanation: The text uses the term 'depth multiplier' while describing changes to the number of channels in the first layer. In standard CNN literature, 'depth' refers to the number of layers, while 'width' or 'channels' refers to the dimensionality of the feature maps. This creates ambiguity about whether you scaled the number of residual blocks or simply the base channel count. If only the channels were changed, 'depth multiplier' is technically incorrect.

comments.explanation: The text describes an interpolation in the log domain but does not explicitly state which boundary v=0 and v=1 correspond to. In Equation 16, v=1 yields beta_t (the upper bound) and v=0 yields beta_tilde_t (the lower bound). This is consistent with the math but can be confusing if a reader assumes v is a mixing coefficient where 0 is the primary state. coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents several modifications to Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs), specifically focusing on improving log-likelihood estimation and sampling efficiency. The authors introduce a learned variance parameterization, a hybrid training objective, and a cosine-based noise schedule to address limitations in the original DDPM formulation.

The paper provides a significant and well-executed set of improvements to the DDPM framework, successfully bridging the gap between high-quality sample generation and competitive log-likelihood estimation. The empirical results are convincing, and the proposed techniques, particularly the cosine schedule and hybrid objective, are highly relevant for practical deployment of diffusion models.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Justification for the Cosine Schedule Shape: In Section 3.2, the authors introduce a cosine-based noise schedule defined by Equation 18. While the empirical performance improvement is clear, the paper lacks a formal or intuitive justification for why this specific functional form is superior to other potential schedules that might also mitigate the rapid destruction of information. The authors admit the choice is arbitrary, but without a comparative analysis of different functional forms (e.g., sigmoid or other polynomial schedules), it remains unclear if the cosine shape is a fundamental requirement or merely a specific instance of a broader class of successful schedules. A brief ablation or theoretical discussion on the desired properties of the schedule (e.g., the rate of change of signal-to-noise ratio) would strengthen the contribution.

- Sensitivity of the Hybrid Objective to Hyperparameters: The hybrid objective defined in Equation 17 introduces a weighting factor lambda = 0.001 to balance the variational lower bound and the simplified objective. The paper does not provide an ablation study on the sensitivity of this hyperparameter, leaving the reader to wonder if this value is universal or dataset-dependent. Given that the hybrid objective is a central contribution for achieving competitive log-likelihoods, demonstrating that the results are not overly sensitive to this choice is necessary. Including a sensitivity analysis or a discussion on the trade-off between the two objectives would clarify the robustness of the proposed method.

- Lack of Discussion on Sampling Artifacts at Low Step Counts: Section 4 discusses reducing the number of sampling steps to improve efficiency, showing that 100 steps can achieve near-optimal FID. However, the paper does not qualitatively or quantitatively address potential artifacts or loss of diversity that might arise when using these reduced step counts. While FID is a useful metric, it is known to be insensitive to certain types of mode collapse or subtle visual artifacts. A more thorough analysis of visual quality, perhaps through a user study or specific metrics for diversity, would be appropriate to support the claim that faster sampling does not degrade generative quality.

- Limited Scalability Discussion for High-Resolution Data: The paper focuses primarily on ImageNet 64x64 and CIFAR-10. While the authors suggest that diffusion models have excellent scaling properties in Section 5, the computational cost of the proposed hybrid objective and the learned variance parameterization for higher resolutions (e.g., 256x256 or 512x512) is not addressed. As the field moves toward higher-resolution synthesis, it is important to know if the learned variance parameterization remains stable or if the importance sampling strategy for time steps becomes computationally prohibitive. A discussion on the potential bottlenecks for scaling to higher resolutions would provide a more complete picture of the method's utility.

- Absence of empirical validation for learned variance stability: The paper introduces a learned variance parameterization in Section 3.1, yet it lacks an empirical demonstration of the training stability or convergence behavior of this component compared to fixed variance heuristics. While the paper claims this parameterization allows for more flexible modeling, it does not show the evolution of the learned variance values over the course of training. To address this, the authors should provide a plot showing the learned variance trajectories across different time steps $t$ and demonstrate that these values do not oscillate or collapse to the boundaries of the interpolation range during the training process.

- Lack of quantitative evidence for generative diversity at low step counts: The authors claim that their model allows for an order of magnitude faster sampling with only a modest difference in quality, relying primarily on FID as the metric. FID is notoriously insensitive to mode dropping and other forms of restricted diversity. To substantiate the claim that the model maintains generative diversity at low sampling steps, the authors should report Precision and Recall metrics or provide a comparison of the distribution of sampled images against the ground truth training set using a measure like the Coverage Metric. This would provide the necessary evidence that the faster sampling process does not inadvertently collapse onto a subset of the data distribution.

- Missing implementation details for importance sampling dynamic updates: The importance sampling technique described in Section 3.3 relies on a history of the previous 10 values for each loss term to update $p_t$ dynamically. The paper does not specify how this initialization phase or the subsequent updates affect the training stability, particularly in the early stages when the loss terms are highly volatile. A brief discussion or a figure showing the evolution of the sampling distribution $p_t$ over the course of training would be necessary to guide practitioners on how to implement this effectively. Without this, the reproducibility of the importance sampling improvement remains questionable.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a brief ablation or sensitivity analysis for the lambda hyperparameter in the hybrid objective.

- Add a qualitative discussion or visual examples comparing samples generated with 4000 steps versus 100 steps to address potential artifacts.

- Provide a brief discussion on the expected computational overhead of the learned variance parameterization when scaling to higher resolutions.

comments.explanation: The claim that WaveGrad's sampling efficiency is primarily due to mel-spectrogram conditioning is misleading. The core innovation in that work is the use of a learned noise schedule and a training objective that enables non-Markovian sampling trajectories, which are the primary drivers of their efficiency. Attributing their performance to the conditioning signal ignores the algorithmic contributions that are analogous to your own improvements.
